coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots enhance and optimize the photoluminescence of micro-spherical YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunyang
Du, Xiaodi
Shi, Yurong
Huang, Jingbin
Jin, Lin
Wang, Zhenling
Zhang, Xingcai
coredata.dc:description: Lanthanide (Ln)-doped YBO3 phosphors are regarded as vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) materials because the position of the O2+→Ln3+ charge transfer band (CTB) locates in the deep ultraviolet (DUV) region (100–280 nm). The applications of Ln-doped YBO3 will be further extended if they are excited in the near ultraviolet (NUV) region such as 350–420 nm. By mixing carbon dots (CDs) and YBO3:Eu3+, the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was successfully fabricated and exhibits a broad excitation band with the strongest peak around 370 nm, which matches the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of the current mainstream LED chip. In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the composite dramatically increased as compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+, which could be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+. It is worth noting that the PL spectrum of the composite is mainly composed of two parts: blue and red light emissions, originating from the emission of CDs and Eu3+ ions, respectively. Therefore, we believe that CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite materials have great potential as a phosphor for the plant-growth light and other applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rare earth ions
Phosphor
Luminescence properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 51572303 ), Young Scientists Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702378 ), Key Technologies R & D Program of Henan Province (No. 172102210090 ), Henan Province Key Discipline of Applied Chemistry (No. 201218692 ), Innovation Scientists and Technicians Troop Construction Projects of Henan Province (No. 2013259 ), Foundation of Henan Educational Committee (No. 15A150085 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors with different amounts of Eu3+ ions (a), and PL spectra of the YBO3:9.0 mol% Eu3+ phosphor (b).
Energy level diagram of YBO3:Eu3+ phosphor.
PL excitation and emission spectra (a) and energy level structures (b) of CDs.
TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the as-prepared CDs.
SEM images of the prepared YBO3:Eu3+ (a) and CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite (b and c).
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of the prepared CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite.

Schematic illustration for the formation of the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite: (a) The amorphous particles are formed in the supersaturated solution; (b) The nanoflakes of CDs@ YBO3:Eu3+ begin to grow after the amorphous particles dissolve; (c) The nanoflakes are arrayed into a spherical structure; (d) The compact spherical of CDs@ YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors are formed as the reaction time increases.

PL excitation (yellow lines, λem = 611 nm) and emission (green lines, λex = 370 nm; red lines, λex = 254 nm) spectra of the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite, and PL emission (blue lines, λex = 370 nm; black lines, λex = 254 nm) spectra of YBO3:Eu3+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
A schematic illustration of the energy transfer mechanism of CDs to Eu3+.
Decay curves of YBO3:Eu3+ and CDs@YBO3:Eu3+.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots enhance and optimize the photoluminescence of micro-spherical YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications, Zhoukou Normal University, Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan, Zhoukou, 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications
Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials
International Joint Research Laboratory for Biomedical Nanomaterials of Henan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, Harvard University, Cambridge, MA, 02138, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide (Ln)-doped YBO3 phosphors are regarded as vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) materials because the position of the O2+→Ln3+ charge transfer band (CTB) locates in the deep ultraviolet (DUV) region (100–280 nm). The applications of Ln-doped YBO3 will be further extended if they are excited in the near ultraviolet (NUV) region such as 350–420 nm. By mixing carbon dots (CDs) and YBO3:Eu3+, the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was successfully fabricated and exhibits a broad excitation band with the strongest peak around 370 nm, which matches the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of the current mainstream LED chip. In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the composite dramatically increased as compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+, which could be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+. It is worth noting that the PL spectrum of the composite is mainly composed of two parts: blue and red light emissions, originating from the emission of CDs and Eu3+ ions, respectively. Therefore, we believe that CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite materials have great potential as a phosphor for the plant-growth light and other applications.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, it is important to obtain a light source that phosphors are pumped by high-efficiency ultraviolet (UV) and near UV (NUV) light-emitting diodes (LEDs), which operate in the wavelength range of 350–420 nm [1]. The use of the Lanthanide (Ln)-doped host materials is one of the most important methods to obtain desired phosphors [2,3]. Recently, phosphors based on Ln-doped REBO3 (RE = Y, La, Gd and Lu) have attracted much attention due to the strong photoluminescence (PL) intensity [4,5]. As a star material in borates, YBO3 has drawn great attentions because of their high stability, low toxicity, strong luminescence intensity and exceptional optical damage threshold as compared with that of other hosts [4–9]. However, Ln-doped REBO3 are usually regarded as vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) materials because the position of the O2+→Ln3+ charge transfer band (CTB) is located in the deep ultraviolet (DUV) region (100–280 nm) [4], and this seriously limits their applications. Therefore, it is very essential to extend the excitation wavelength of Ln-doped REBO3 to the range of 350–420 nm. Among trivalent rare earth ions, Eu3+ ion is regarded as a promising red-emitting activator for LEDs since its narrow red emission originating from 5D0→7F2 transition [10–13]. It is revealed that the Eu3+ doped phosphors can emit bright red emission under UV light.

As we know, the luminescence characteristics of materials strongly affected by their homogeneity, particle size, defects and so on. Therefore, it is crucial to achieve the synthesis of phosphors with controllable size and morphology [14,15]. Generally, liquid phase methods such as a hydrothermal approach can resolve these problems, because they provide numerous possible variables that can control size and morphology of the as-prepared materials, such as solvent type, reaction temperature, pH value, preparation time, etc [16,17]. However, luminescent materials prepared by a hydrothermal method usually have a relatively weak PL intensity.

As an emerging star in carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn great attentions since they were first developed in 2004 for their strong and highly tunable PL, small size, biocompatibility, and their ease to be functionalized with biomolecules, etc [18–21]. Studies show that the PL properties of the original materials will be improved when they are combined with CDs [22–24]. However, search demonstrating that CDs enhance and optimize the PL of Ln-doped REBO3 are scarce. In this work, the micro-spherical CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite materials were successfully fabricated, showing a broad excitation band with the strongest peak at about 370 nm, matching the emission wavelength of the current mainstream LED chip. Furthermore, the PL intensity of the composite is dramatically increased as compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+. It is worth noting that the PL of the achieved composite materials consists of both a broad emission band and a series of sharp emission peaks, which are the emission band (the peak at 420 nm) of CDs and the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ ions, respectively. It is well known that among environmental factors affecting crop yield and quality, light is a crucial factor because it is an energy source for photosynthesis. According to the previous reports, combination of red (600–700 nm) and blue (400–500 nm) light has been proven to be an effective lighting source for producing spinach, radish, pepper, Swiss chard, wheat, and lettuce biomass in controlled environments [25]. Therefore, we believe that the composite has great potential to be used as a phosphor for plant-growth light and other applications.

The synthesis of YBO3 samples via the hydrothermal approach can be described as follows: Firstly, stoichiometric amounts of Y2O3 (99.9% purity) and Eu2O3 (99.9% purity) were dissolved in aqueous HCl and HNO3 solutions, and HBO3 (99.9% purity) was dissolved in deionized water and obtained 0.1 M YCl3, Eu(NO3)3 and HBO3 solution, respectively. Secondly, a certain amount 0.1 M Eu(NO3)3 solution, 15.0 mL YCl3 and 30.0 mL HBO3 solutions were mixed and stirred for 30 min, and the mixed solution was rapidly adjusted to a designated pH = 8 using ammonia water (25%) and stirred constantly for 30 min. Thirdly, the mixed suspension was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner of 100 mL capacity, sealed, and heated at 180 °C for 26 h. Finally, as the autoclave cooled to room temperature naturally, the precipitates were separated by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol for six times, and then dried in a vacuum oven at about 60 °C for 12 h, and YBO3:Eu3+ phosphor was successfully fabricated. By controlling the amount of Eu(NO3)3 solution, YBO3:x mol%Eu3+ (x = 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0 and 11.0) phosphors were prepared.

The CDs were synthesized according to the previously reported method, using citric acid as the carbon source in the presence of ethylenediamine [26]. The synthesis procedure of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was similar to YBO3:Eu3+ except that 0.3 mL of CDs solution was added in the mixed solution during the second step when YBO3:9.0 mol%Eu3+ was fabricating.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the products were recorded on a Bruker D8 Focus Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100 Plus TEM at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken using a FEI Quanta 200 SEM. PL spectra were carried out using an FLS920P Edinburgh Analytical Instrument apparatus equipped with a 450 W Xenon lamp and a μF900H high-energy microsecond flash lamp as the excitation sources. All the measurements were performed at room temperature.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of the as-obtained Eu3+-doped YBO3. All diffraction peaks of the as-obtained samples can be readily indexed to the pure hexagonal phase of the YBO3 crystal [space group: P63/m] with lattice constants a = b = 0.37796 nm, c = 0.8816 nm, according to the JCPDS file No. 16–0277 [5]. There are other impurity peaks which are found at around 21.3 and 24.8° for two theta when the amount of Eu3+ doping concentration reaches to 11.0 mol%, and this can be caused by excess Eu3+ ions. Fig. 1b exhibits the excitation and emission spectra of YBO3:9.0 mol%Eu3+ phosphors. The excitation spectrum consists of the 4f-4f intraconfiguration transitions of Eu3+ (for example: 394 nm, 7F0→5L6; 467 nm, 7F0→5D2) and the O2−→Eu3+ charge transfer band (CTB) in the range of 170–250 nm [27–29]. The characteristic emission peaks at 580, 592, 611/627 and 652 nm are attributed to the transitions from 5D0 level to 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2 and 3) levels of Eu3+ [30,31], as shown in Fig. 2 [4]. When excited by DUV at 254 nm, electrons in the ground state are excited to the charge transfer (CT) state. The electrons in the CT state can transfer the excited energy to the level of 5D3 or higher levels in Eu3+ through a resonance process. The energy nonradiatively relaxes from these high energy levels to the 5D0 level by multiphonon relaxation [27], and then the characteristic emissions of Eu3+ transitions occur. The major emissions are at 592 nm corresponding to 5D0→7F1 transition which is due to the typical electric dipole transition, and 611 nm and 627 nm corresponding to 5D0→7F2 transition which is attributed to the magnetic dipole transition, respectively [27,32,33]. Moreover, it is noting that the PL integral intensity increases as the Eu3+ doping concentration in YBO3 increases from 3.0 to 9.0 mol%, and then decreases due to the concentration quenching when the doping concentration is up to 11.0 mol% under the 254 nm excitation (Fig. S1). Therefore, YBO3:9.0 mol%Eu3+ (YBO3:Eu3+) is chosen as an object of study.

The XRD pattern (Fig. S2) of the as-prepared CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors is in agreement with the pure hexagonal phase of the YBO3 crystal, and the peaks of CDs are not found due to the low content. Fig. 5 a shows the SEM image of the core-shell structured YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors, and the diameter of YBO3:Eu3+ is in the range of 6–8 μm. Recently, the core-shell structure has been verified as the good method to enhance the fluorescence intensity [41–43]. By mixing the CDs and YBO3:Eu3+, the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was fabricated and the low magnification SEM of the composite is shown in Fig. 5b that the phosphors have regular sphere shape with a diameter of about 14 μm, and it also shows uniform distribution. Simultaneously, some of them still maintain the core-shell structure. As mentioned above, the diameter of as-prepared CDs is mainly in the range of 2.5–5.0 nm. As a result, the volume of the as-prepared YBO3:Eu3+ is 1010 times more than that of the CDs, so that CDs cannot easily destroy the morphology and structure of the as-prepared CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors. Further, the higher magnification SEM image (Fig. 5c) reveals that the individual phosphor microsphere is composed of nanoflakes.

The internal structure of the as-prepared CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was analyzed with TEM after ultrasonic treatment 60 s. As mentioned above, the composite is composed of nanoflakes, which can also be confirmed by the TEM image (Fig. 6 a). Obviously, the nanoflakes have an irregular shape and the size is about hundreds of nanometers. The thinner region of the nanoflakes is further observed by HRTEM micrograph (Fig. 6b). This image indicates the average value of the interplanar spacing of as-prepared composite is 0.325 nm, which corresponds to (10-10) planes of YBO3:Eu3+, and further analysis indicates that YBO3:Eu3+ nanoflakes grow along the [10–10], [-1100] or [01–10] crystallographic directions [5,44]. Typically, CDs contain many carboxylic acid moieties at their surface, leading to the excellent water solubility and the suitability for subsequent functionalization with various organic, polymeric, inorganic, or biological species [45,46]. In Fig. 6b, it can be seen that the individual CD tightly adheres with the nanoflake of YBO3:Eu3+ which can be ascribed to the interaction between carboxylic acid moieties and YBO3:Eu3+, thus we cannot distinguish the boundaries between CD and the nanoflake of YBO3:Eu3+. However, the lattice planes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.208 nm can be clearly identified (inner of the green circle in Fig. 6b), and it is just corresponding to the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon as mentioned above.

A schematic image of the proposed growth process is illustrated in Fig. 7 . Firstly, the amorphous particles are formed in the supersaturated solution and begin to dissolve with the reaction temperature increases. Secondly, the precipitate of YBO3:Eu3+ appears and becomes the nuclei (Fig. 7a), the freshly formed nuclei serving as the crystal nucleus are thermodynamically unstable owing to the high surface energy, and grow rapidly into nanoflakes (Fig. 7b), and the interaction, which exists between carboxylic acid moieties at their surface of CDs and YBO3:Eu3+, combines them into CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite. Thirdly, the nanoflakes are arrayed by the rolling solution with a high temperature and pressure and the spherical CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite gradually grows up due to the spherical structure has the lowest surface energy (Fig. 7c) [47,48]. And the surfaces of the nanoflakes are very smooth, owing to the Ostwald ripening process. Lastly, the composite becomes more and more compact as the reaction time increases (Fig. 7d). Briefly, the growth of hierarchical structure is considered as an initial nucleating, in situ self-assembled, and the Ostwald ripening process [49,50].

The optimized performance of the as-prepared CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite is shown in Fig. 8 , and YBO3:Eu3+ phosphor is used as a reference. Obviously, the excitation spectrum of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite changed greatly compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+ (Fig. 1b). The CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite shows a broad excitation band at a range of 240–440 nm with the strongest peak at 370 nm which matches with the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of the current mainstream LED chip. In other words, the VUV YBO3:Eu3+ becomes a NUV material when it is mixed with CDs. It will have a more extensive range of applications in the field of luminescence materials. In addition, no matter what excitation wavelength is used, the integrated PL intensity of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite is dramatically increased compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. Theoretically, there are three primary conditions for FRET: Firstly, donor and acceptor molecules must be in close proximity (typically 1–10 nm). Secondly, the absorption spectrum of the acceptor must overlap the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor. Thirdly, donor and acceptor transition dipole orientations must be approximately parallel [51,52]. As mentioned above, CDs and YBO3:Eu3+ are in very close proximity even though the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite was measured by the HRTEM (Fig. 6b), therefore we cannot distinguish their boundaries. Furthermore, because the absorption spectrum of Eu3+ ions (Fig. 1b) is 260–500 nm and the emission spectrum of the CDs is 380–550 nm (Fig. 8), there clearly exists an overlap between the absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum. Therefore, we conclude that there exists FRET between CDs and Eu3+ ions, as shown in Fig. 9 [53,54]. Moreover, it is worth noting that the PL of composite materials is composed of both a broad emission band and a series of sharp emission peak, which are the emission band (the peak at 420 nm) of CDs and the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ ions, respectively .

The luminescent decay curve of YBO3:Eu3+ and CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite can be well fitted into the following double exponential function (Fig. 10 ) [27]:
(1)
I =
I
1
e x p ( - t /
τ
1
) +
I
2
e x p ( - t /
τ
2
)
where I
1 and I
2 are the intensities at different time intervals, τ
1 and τ
2 are their corresponding lifetimes. During the fitting, the values of the parameter goodness are 0.9995 for YBO3:Eu3+ and 0.9956 for CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite, respectively, indicating that the double exponential fitting is satisfactory. The average lifetime τ
AV can be calculated using the following equation [27]:
(2)
τ
A V
=
(
I
1
τ
1
2
+
I
1
τ
1
2
)
/
(
I
1
τ
1
+
I
1
τ
1
)
and the values are 0.27 ms for YBO3:Eu3+ and 1.63 ms for CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite, respectively. Obviously, the lifetime of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ greatly increases as compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+, demonstrating that there exists FRET between CDs and Eu3+ ions. What's more, the quantum efficiency is 14.5% for YBO3:Eu3+ and 21.2% for CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ under the excitation of 370 nm, which was measured according to the method as reported [55]. Certainly, the quantum efficiency of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ increases, which also attributes to the FRET between CDs and Eu3+ ions.

In this work, Eu3+ ions doped YBO3 phosphors have been successfully prepared via a hydrothermal method, and the PL integral intensity of phosphors reached the maximum value with the amount of Eu3+ doping concentration of 9.0 mol%. In addition, well-dispersed spherical CDs have also been fabricated via a hydrothermal method. Further, CDs@ YBO3:Eu3+ composite materials were synthesized and their PL intensity was dramatically increased compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+. The PL of composite materials consists of both a broad emission band and a series of sharp emission peaks, which are the emission band (the peak at 420 nm) of CDs and the characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ ions, respectively. We attribute the mechanism of this phenomenon to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and Eu3+ ions. What's more, the composite materials have a broad excitation band with the peak at 370 nm, which well matched the mainstream emission wavelength of the LED. We conclude that the composite materials can be used as a phosphor for plant-growth light and other applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 51572303), Young Scientists Fund of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702378), Key Technologies R & D Program of Henan Province (No. 172102210090), Henan Province Key Discipline of Applied Chemistry (No. 201218692), Innovation Scientists and Technicians Troop Construction Projects of Henan Province (No. 2013259), Foundation of Henan Educational Committee (No. 15A150085).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.12.383.
